---
title: "Epidemiology & Health Sciences Journal Club"
summary: Journal club
output:
  html_document:
    keep_md: false
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    css: style_3.css

---

<br>

## Next session

**Date:** May 1 at 13h (CET) — [sign up below](#subscribe-to-the-mailing-list) to receive a link to the meeting

**Paper:** Mendelian Methodology: Is this the end of the Mendelian era?



<!-- ![](epidemiology x.png) -->
<!-- [![](paper_screenshot.png)](https://jech.bmj.com/content/80/4/205#ref-12) -->

<br>

---

## Winter/Spring 2026 Schedule

| Date | Reference | Paper |
|:---|:---|:---|
| January 23 | Discussion | Are association questions just causal questions in disguise? |
| February 6 | Westreich 2017 | [From patients to policy](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5453818/) |
| February 20 | van den Broek | Does SES affect long term health-related quality of life in patients with traumatic rib fractures? |
| March 6 | Azizi | Investigating IgG N-glycosylation changes in relation to cancer development |
| March 20 | Oberndorfer et al. 2026 | [Descriptive estimands, causal estimands, and avoiding the jungle of adjusted associations 'in between'](https://jech.bmj.com/content/80/4/205#ref-12) |
| April 17 | Al-kassab-Córdova & Soto-Becerra 2025 | [Causal Inference in Public Health: A Call to Stop Causal Fishing](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12237941/) |
| May 1 | Dagelinckx | Mendelian Methodology: Is this the end of the Mendelian era? |
| May 15 | | |
| May 29 | | |
| June 12 | | |

[→ Previous sessions](https://www.jeremylabrecque.org/journal_club/previous_sessions)

<br>

---

## Subscribe to the mailing list

[Sign up here to receive meeting links and updates.](https://forms.gle/764GgYN3bPek9c5S8)

<br>

---

## Zotero library

All papers can be found in [this public Zotero library](https://www.zotero.org/groups/5487300/emc_journal_club/collections/5G9UYTEY). You can browse the library in your browser. If you have a Zotero account, [you can add the library to your own Zotero by clicking "Join" here](https://www.zotero.org/groups/5487300/emc_journal_club).

<br>


---

## The idea behind journal club

I just wanted to take a few minutes to write down a bit about the philosophy behind journal club. The main idea behind the journal club in our department is to have a place to discuss ideas, whether new or old, in an atmosphere that is open and inclusive. A place where anyone can participate, there are no wrong or stupid questions, and we move together as a group to better understand the topic of discussion.

I also want to add that there is also benefit to attending even if you don't fully understand the topic or speak during the meeting. I encountered many epi concepts for the first time in a journal club and, while I might have left with only a vague understanding, when I did re-encounter the concept later in the classroom, I was already familiar with it making it easier to learn. So I encourage attendance even just to observe the conversation.

It's also a great place to sharpen your "thinking on your feet" and scientific creativity skills. The conversations in journal club don't tend to always go where we expect them to and I love hearing new ideas or new perspectives on old ideas — I don't know where I would encounter those ideas otherwise!

Lastly, journal club can expose you to new topics. I discovered the topic, method and dataset that I used in my PhD at the social epidemiology journal club at McGill (in different sessions), so you never know what you're going to come away with when you attend.

Let me know if you have any ideas for the journal club. Looking forward to future meetings!

*Journal club helps you use concepts you learn in class*

After my undergrad I was accepted to a 6-month internship in Brazil. I spoke absolutely no Portuguese and Duolingo had not yet been invented. But because no one spoke English in my environment, I picked up the ability to speak easily in Portuguese in 3 months.

Switch to the present day and despite having been in the Netherlands for more than six years now, my Dutch is still pretty poor despite completing two Dutch courses and finishing the Duolingo course multiple times. I can have basic conversations but it's hard for me to converse about anything beyond the weather.

Why this difference? Because in Brazil I was forced to speak and listen every day. I was _using_ Portuguese, not just learning it. For Dutch, I learned almost everything I need to know to be able to converse, but I haven't used what I learned because everyone speaks English here — so I'm never forced to use it! This makes all the difference in the world.

Learning new concepts in epidemiology or on any topic works the same way. You can learn concepts in class and even get great grades. But if you don't use what you learn, you don't develop the same kind of expertise. Taking classes is the first step of the learning process, but using that knowledge as much as possible is what will make it easier to apply these concepts in daily practice and make you an expert.
